headline: Coastal Erosion Threatens 1,400km of Malaysian Shoreline
context: NAHRIM's 2025 national coastal vulnerability assessment found 1,400km of Malaysian coastline — 29% of the total — now classified as critically or severely eroding.

cards.big: Malaysia's coastline is 4,800km long. 1,400km is actively eroding.
cards.sub: That is 29% of all shoreline — up from 18% in the 2015 assessment.

cards.big: Sand mining removed 312 million tonnes from coastal areas since 2010.
cards.sub: Sediment deficit is the primary driver of erosion at 62% of affected sites.

cards.big: JPS spent RM3.2B on coastal protection between 2015 and 2024.
cards.sub: 48% of rock revetments built before 2018 have already failed or require major repair.

cards.big: 156,000 people live within 200m of critically eroding coastline.
cards.sub: No national coastal retreat or managed realignment policy exists.

cards.big: Building walls against the sea while removing the sand that forms the coast is self-defeating.
Malaysia needs a national coastal policy that integrates sand budgets, development setbacks, and managed retreat. Seawalls alone cannot hold a coastline that is structurally starved of sediment.
